identity.title: Corrugated and Solid Fiber Box Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Independent US corrugated and solid-fiber box manufacturers in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly design, convert, print, and supply boxes to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive packaging plants, containerboard mills without box converting, brokers without accountable production, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring business-to-business sales of custom or standard corrugated boxes under quotes, purchase orders, supply agreements, and recurring replenishment programs, commonly priced per thousand square feet or per unit with board and freight adjustments.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts describe all paper manufacturing rather than a wage-weighted task inventory for 322211. The mix was translated by treating order entry, estimating, structural-design support, scheduling, procurement, quality paperwork, sales administration, and maintenance knowledge work as exposed while excluding most machine operation, material handling, and physical maintenance; broader-sector data may understate customer-service work in converters.
a.rationale: The broader sector is dominated by paper-goods machine operators, supervisors, truck operators, and production managers, so most wages support physical converting. Corrugated plants also have repeat, information-dense quoting, design, order, scheduling, and service workflows where AI can substitute tasks or avoid incremental hiring.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries from a representative set of independent corrugated plants, separating already automated CAD/ERP tasks from manual estimating, order entry, scheduling, customer service, quality, maintenance, and production work.
caveats: Occupation evidence is NAICS 322 and not specific to corrugated plants.
Current ERP, CAD, and plant-automation penetration is unknown, creating risk of counting already automated work.

rho.rationale: High-volume estimating, specification intake, artwork checks, order entry, scheduling, procurement, service triage, and maintenance support can be integrated over five years. Customer-specific tolerances, ERP and CAD fragmentation, food-contact requirements, production downtime risk, and human approval reduce full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Audited multi-plant deployments showing task-level labor hours, error rates, scrap, on-time delivery, and headcount avoidance before and after AI-assisted quoting, scheduling, artwork review, and service automation.
caveats: No representative AI implementation study for 322211 was found.
Implementation can shift work to exception handling rather than eliminate it.

e.rationale: Independent corrugated converters ordinarily serve external customers on a repeat basis and many plants can operate as transferable businesses. Captive facilities, divisions embedded in integrated paper groups, customer-dependent plants, thinly capitalized operators, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band are excluded.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the frozen n population with ownership, captive status, external revenue, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, lease and equipment condition, and stand-alone management depth.
caveats: The injected n=212 uses an estimated EBITDA margin bridge and can misclassify low- or high-margin converters.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner ages across responding US employer businesses, not corrugated-company sale events. The 51% age-55-plus share was converted into a five-year qualifying-transfer probability after allowing for family succession, continued ownership, closures, and ordinary strategic sales; consolidation interest could make the all-industry proxy understate sales, while integrated family firms could make it overstate them.
s5.rationale: General owner aging creates succession pressure and corrugated plants have tangible assets and recurring accounts that can support transfer. Not every succession event becomes an external control transaction, and customer concentration or equipment needs can prevent a sale.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction and failed-process database for independent US corrugated manufacturers in the EBITDA band over the last decade, paired with current owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
caveats: The Census age distribution is all-industry and data year 2018.
Age is a succession-pressure proxy, not an observed transfer rate.

q.rationale: Converters can retain gains from faster quotes, lower order errors, reduced setup waste, better scheduling, and improved service, especially in short-run custom work. Competitive bids, board-price formulas, annual reviews, and large customers' purchasing leverage pass part of recurring savings back through price.
q.change_evidence: Representative customer contracts and quote histories showing board-cost pass-through, repricing cadence, win-loss behavior, and gross-margin persistence after measured operational improvements.
caveats: No contract-level retention sample was available.
Retention varies between commodity brown-box volume and high-service custom displays or short runs.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all converted paper products, not 322211 quantity, at 1.7% annual growth through 2034. Census measures nominal retail e-commerce sales rather than box demand, and AF&PA measures upstream containerboard production and utilization. Combining slower mature-box demand with these positive drivers may overstate demand if packaging reduction offsets shipments or understate it if e-commerce and substitution from plastic accelerate.
d5.rationale: BLS projects converted-paper-product real output rising from $99.0 billion in 2024 to $116.9 billion in 2034. E-commerce sales were 16.9% of retail and grew 9.8% year over year in 2026 Q1, supporting shipment-related boxes, while 2025 containerboard output fell 4.4% despite a 91.9% operating rate; the balanced five-year case is modest growth.
d5.change_evidence: Fibre Box Association quarterly US box shipments and square-foot production by end market, reconciled with announced capacity, lightweighting, box-size reduction, and real customer shipment volumes.
caveats: E-commerce sales are nominal and not a direct measure of corrugated use.
BLS output is NAICS 3222, while upstream AF&PA containerboard data include uses beyond 322211.

o.rationale: Boxes are physical, customer-specific shipping containers that must be designed, converted, printed, quality-checked, staged, and delivered by an accountable manufacturer. Digital ordering and automated equipment reduce labor and transaction friction but do not replace the operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers self-manufacturing boxes at scale or autonomous box plants operating without accountable production, quality, maintenance, and customer-service management.
caveats: On-demand box-making systems may internalize a small portion of standard demand at large customers.
Volume effects from packaging reduction belong in d5 rather than this operator-share estimate.

narrative.executive_view: Corrugated box manufacturing combines a substantial estimated LMM population and recurring local customer relationships with a production-heavy labor base. AI is most credible in quoting, design support, order management, scheduling, procurement, maintenance assistance, and service rather than physical converting.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can structure customer specifications, accelerate quotes, check artwork and orders, assist CAD choices, forecast demand, sequence jobs, surface maintenance knowledge, and automate routine service. Machine setup, feeding, printing, die cutting, gluing, bundling, maintenance, quality release, and material movement remain substantially physical.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits that improve speed, accuracy, scrap, uptime, and customer retention are less visible and more retainable than simple administrative cost cuts. Board-price formulas and competitive rebids nevertheless return a meaningful portion to customers over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: Most independent converters fit repeat external supply, but captive plants and integrated divisions do not. The frozen estimate of 212 band firms is materially uncertain because profitability varies by mix, equipment, and board costs; owner aging provides only broad transfer support.
narrative.demand_durability: E-commerce and physical-goods distribution support box usage, and BLS expects converted-paper output growth. Lightweighting, right-sizing, reusable alternatives, industrial cyclicality, and the 2025 upstream containerboard decline prevent treating online-sales growth as one-for-one box growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are the six-digit occupational mix, baseline automation, independent-versus-captive ownership, margin-bridged firm count, customer concentration, equipment condition, board-price pass-through, and actual square-foot shipment growth. A downturn in goods production can quickly lower plant utilization.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring, specification-rich customer orders create implementable AI workflows across quoting, scheduling, quality, maintenance support, and service.
narrative.principal_weakness: Physical production dominates labor and competitive board-linked pricing can pass efficiency gains to powerful customers.

sources.title: Paper Manufacturing: NAICS 322
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 paper-manufacturing employment of 87,050 paper-goods machine operators, 17,540 production supervisors, 14,210 industrial-truck operators, and 6,640 production managers, evidencing a production-heavy labor mix.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects converted paper product manufacturing output from $99.0 billion in 2024 to $116.9 billion in 2034 in chained 2017 dollars, a 1.7% compound annual growth rate, while employment falls 5.4%.

sources.title: Quarterly Retail E-Commerce Sales, First Quarter 2026
sources.supports: Census reports seasonally adjusted US retail e-commerce sales of $326.7 billion in 2026 Q1, up 9.8% from 2025 Q1 and equal to 16.9% of total retail sales.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, based on 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.

sources.title: AF&PA Releases 66th Annual Paper Industry Capacity and Fiber Consumption Survey
sources.supports: AF&PA reports 2025 containerboard production fell 4.4% to 36.1 million tons, capacity declined 5.1%, and operating rate held at 91.9%; its data represent about 87% of US paper and paperboard capacity with estimates completing the dataset.
